title: The Winemaking Guide
subtitle: From crush to cork: the art and science of transforming grapes into wine

overview.title: Understanding Winemaking
overview.description: Winemaking (vinification) is the process of transforming grapes into wine. While viticulture determines the raw material's potential, winemaking realizes that potential - or squanders it. Great winemakers make decisive interventions at critical moments while knowing when to step back and let the wine make itself.

interventionist.title: Interventionist Approach
interventionist.description: Use all available tools to craft a specific style. Technology, additives, and technique shape the wine toward a vision. The winemaker is an artist with complete control over the medium.

non_interventionist.title: Non-Interventionist Approach
non_interventionist.description: Minimal intervention allows terroir and vintage to express themselves. The winemaker is a guide, not a sculptor. 'Natural' wine takes this to its extreme.

philosophy.balance: Most quality producers fall between these extremes, intervening thoughtfully while respecting the fruit's character. The goal is wine that reflects both place and maker.

key_terminology.definition: Freshly crushed grape juice containing skins, seeds, and pulp. The raw material for fermentation.
The layer of skins, seeds, and pulp that floats atop fermenting red wine. Must be managed for extraction.
Dead yeast cells and grape particles that settle after fermentation. Can add texture and complexity if stirred.
Transferring wine off its sediment to a clean vessel. Clarifies wine and introduces controlled oxygen.
Bacterial conversion of sharp malic acid to softer lactic acid. Adds creaminess and stability.
Sulfur dioxide (SO2) added to prevent oxidation and microbial spoilage. Used since Roman times.
Measurement of sugar content in grape juice. Determines potential alcohol level.
Acetic acid and its esters. Low levels add complexity; high levels indicate spoilage.

reception.title: Harvest Reception
reception.description: The winemaking process begins the moment grapes arrive at the winery. Speed, temperature, and careful handling in these first hours set the trajectory for the entire vintage.

stages.name: Arrival & Inspection
stages.description: Grapes arrive at the winery by truck, trailer, or small bins. Quick assessment determines processing decisions.
considerations: Time since picking (minimizing delay is critical)
stages.best_practice: Process grapes within hours of picking. Night harvesting keeps fruit cool. Small bins prevent crushing under weight.

stages.name: Sorting
stages.description: Removing damaged, unripe, or rotten fruit before processing. Critical for quality wines.

manual.description: Workers visually inspect and remove unwanted material on a sorting table
advantages: Can sort for color, size, damage

optical.description: Cameras and air jets automatically eject defective berries

density.description: Berries float in salt water; ripe berries sink, unripe float

stages.when_to_sort: Sorting is most critical when fruit quality is variable. Top estates sort meticulously; volume producers may skip entirely.

stages.name: Destemming
stages.description: Separating grape berries from their stems. Stems can add tannin and green flavors if included.

partial_stems.description: Some percentage of whole clusters retained
partial_stems.effect: Adds structure, spice, and freshness; common in Burgundy (30-100%)

whole_cluster.effect: Carbonic maceration character, lifted aromatics, firm tannins

regional_traditions.burgundy: Increasing whole cluster use, especially for Pinot Noir (30-100%)
regional_traditions.bordeaux: Traditionally full destemming; some experiments with whole cluster
regional_traditions.rhone: Historically whole cluster; modern producers vary widely
regional_traditions.beaujolais: Whole cluster essential for carbonic maceration

stages.name: Crushing
stages.description: Breaking grape skins to release juice. The degree of crushing affects extraction and texture.

hard_crush.description: Berries fully broken, maximum juice release

gentle_crush.description: Skins just broken, berries remain largely intact

no_crush.description: Whole berries placed in tank intact

stages.white_vs_red: White grapes are often pressed immediately after crushing; red grapes go to tank for maceration.

fermentation.title: Fermentation
fermentation.description: Fermentation is the magical transformation where yeast converts sugar to alcohol, carbon dioxide, and hundreds of flavor compounds. It is both the most critical and most dangerous phase of winemaking.

techniques.name: Alcoholic Fermentation
techniques.description: Yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) metabolizes grape sugars into ethanol and CO2. This primary fermentation typically takes 1-4 weeks depending on temperature and yeast activity.

stages.exponential: Rapid yeast growth, vigorous fermentation (2-5 days)
stages.decline: Sugar depletes, yeast dies off, fermentation slows

techniques.completion: Fermentation is complete when sugar reaches 0-2 g/L (dry) or desired residual level. Stuck fermentation is a serious problem.

techniques.name: Yeast Selection
techniques.description: The choice between indigenous (wild) and commercial yeast is one of winemaking's most debated decisions.

indigenous.description: Native yeasts from vineyard and winery environment

commercial.description: Selected strains chosen for specific properties

techniques.name: Temperature Control
techniques.description: Fermentation temperature dramatically affects wine character. Controlling temperature is one of modern winemaking's greatest advances.

cool_fermentation.effect: Preserves aromatics, slow extraction, common for whites

warm_fermentation.effect: Faster extraction, more tannin, richer body
warm_fermentation.risks: Can kill yeast above 35°C, volatile aromas lost

techniques.name: Cap Management
techniques.description: During red wine fermentation, skins float to form a 'cap' that must be kept moist and in contact with juice for extraction.

punchdown.name: Punchdown (Pigeage)
punchdown.description: Pushing cap down into juice manually or mechanically

pumpover.name: Pumpover (Remontage)
pumpover.description: Pumping juice from bottom over cap

rack_and_return.name: Rack and Return (Delestage)
rack_and_return.description: Draining tank completely, allowing cap to fall, then returning juice
rack_and_return.effect: Strong extraction, removes harsh seed tannins

rotary.name: Rotary Fermenters
rotary.description: Horizontal tanks that rotate, keeping cap in constant contact
rotary.effect: Maximum extraction, efficient but can be harsh

submerged_cap.name: Submerged Cap
submerged_cap.description: Screen holds cap below surface throughout fermentation

techniques.name: Cold Soak (Cold Maceration)
techniques.description: Holding crushed grapes at cold temperatures before fermentation begins. Extracts color and fruit character without tannin.
techniques.effect: Enhanced color, fruit-forward aromatics, softer tannins
techniques.when_to_use: Common for Pinot Noir and varieties where fruit expression is prioritized over structure
techniques.risks: Spoilage if not properly managed; requires SO2 or inert gas

techniques.name: Extended Maceration
techniques.description: Leaving wine on skins after fermentation completes. Continues extraction but character changes.
techniques.effect: Tannin polymerization (softer texture), color stability, added complexity
techniques.when_to_use: Age-worthy reds, particularly Nebbiolo, Cabernet, Syrah
techniques.risks: Over-extraction if fruit quality is poor

pressing.title: Pressing
pressing.description: Pressing separates juice or wine from grape solids. The timing, pressure, and method significantly affect wine quality and yield.

techniques.name: Press Timing
techniques.description: When pressing occurs depends on wine style and grape color.

immediate.name: Direct Press (Whites, Rosé)
immediate.effect: Clean, fresh juice with minimal phenolics

post_fermentation.name: Post-Fermentation (Reds)
post_fermentation.description: Wine pressed after fermentation on skins

pre_fermentation.name: Skin Contact (Orange Wine)
pre_fermentation.effect: Phenolic extraction, amber color, tannic texture

techniques.name: Press Types
techniques.description: Different press designs offer varying levels of extraction and quality.

basket.name: Basket Press (Vertical)
basket.description: Traditional wooden or stainless basket with plate pressing from above

bladder.name: Bladder Press (Pneumatic)
bladder.description: Inflatable bladder inside horizontal cylinder presses fruit against screen

membrane.name: Membrane Press
membrane.description: Similar to bladder but membrane presses inward from all sides

continuous.name: Continuous Press (Screw)
continuous.description: Screw forces fruit through decreasing-diameter tube

techniques.name: Press Fractions
techniques.description: Juice extracted at different pressures has different character.
techniques.usage: Top producers may use only free run. Press fractions can be blended back for structure or sold separately.

mlf.title: Malolactic Fermentation
mlf.description: Malolactic fermentation (MLF) is a bacterial conversion of tart malic acid to softer lactic acid. Despite the name, it's not a true fermentation but a decarboxylation. MLF profoundly affects wine texture, stability, and flavor.

techniques.name: MLF Fundamentals
techniques.description: Lactic acid bacteria (primarily Oenococcus oeni) convert malic acid to lactic acid, releasing CO2.
techniques.timing: Usually occurs after alcoholic fermentation, either spontaneously or by inoculation

techniques.name: Encouraging MLF
techniques.description: Steps to promote malolactic fermentation when desired.

techniques.name: Preventing MLF
techniques.description: Steps to block malolactic fermentation when crisp acidity is desired.
methods: Add SO2 immediately after alcoholic fermentation

techniques.name: Diacetyl & Butter
techniques.description: Diacetyl is a byproduct of MLF responsible for buttery aromas.
techniques.chemistry: Produced as an intermediate in bacterial metabolism; metabolized further if bacteria remain active

controlling_levels.more_butter: Rack wine off lees mid-MLF to preserve diacetyl
controlling_levels.less_butter: Allow MLF to complete fully; lees contact metabolizes diacetyl

regional_preferences.new_world: Often embraced in Chardonnay (butter bomb style)

aging.title: Aging & Maturation
aging.description: Aging transforms raw fermented grape juice into complex, integrated wine. Whether in barrel, tank, or bottle, time allows chemical reactions that develop texture, integrate flavors, and build complexity.

techniques.name: Barrel Aging
techniques.description: Aging wine in wooden barrels (typically oak) adds flavor, texture, and allows controlled oxygen exposure.

techniques.name: Lees Aging (Sur Lie)
techniques.description: Keeping wine in contact with dead yeast cells (lees) adds texture and protects from oxidation.

techniques.name: Tank Aging
techniques.description: Aging in neutral vessels (stainless steel, concrete, or used barrels) focuses on fruit purity.

vessels.concrete: Slight texture, some believe terroir connection

techniques.wines: Aromatic whites, fresh rosés, fruit-forward reds

techniques.name: Oxidative Aging
techniques.description: Deliberate oxygen exposure during aging creates specific styles.

examples.sherry: Solera system with headspace, fino/oloroso styles

techniques.effects: Nutty, dried fruit, caramel notes; amber color development

techniques.name: Bottle Aging
techniques.description: Reductive aging in bottle allows slow chemical evolution.
effects: Primary fruit evolves to secondary/tertiary aromas
Color shifts (red: garnet to brick; white: gold to amber)
techniques.requirements: Cork closure (allows micro-oxygen); stable cellar conditions

blending.title: Blending
blending.description: Blending (assemblage) is the art of combining different lots to create a final wine. Whether blending varieties, vineyards, barrels, or vintages, the goal is a whole greater than its parts.

techniques.name: Varietal Blending
techniques.description: Combining different grape varieties to achieve complexity and balance.

bordeaux_red.rationale: Cabernet provides structure, Merlot adds flesh, Cab Franc aromatics, Petit Verdot color

chateauneuf.rationale: Grenache for warmth, Syrah for spice, Mourvedre for structure

champagne.rationale: Chardonnay for elegance, Pinot for body, Meunier for fruit

port.varieties: 80+ varieties permitted; Touriga Nacional, Touriga Franca, Tinta Roriz key
port.rationale: Each contributes specific characteristics to field blend

techniques.name: Lot Blending
techniques.description: Blending different fermentation lots of the same variety for complexity.
reasons: Different vineyard blocks contribute unique character
techniques.process: Trial blends assembled; tasted over time; final blend refined

techniques.name: Vintage Blending
techniques.description: Blending wines from multiple years, common in non-vintage styles.

examples.nv_champagne: Base wine from current year blended with reserve wines from previous vintages
examples.sherry: Solera system continuously blends young and old wines
examples.tawny_port: Multiple vintages blended; age indicated (10, 20, 30, 40 year)

techniques.goal: Maintain consistent house style across years

techniques.name: Blend Trials
techniques.description: The process of determining final blend through systematic tasting.
process: Taste individual lots to understand components
Create small trial blends with different proportions
Taste trials blind and over time
Confirm final blend and scale to production
techniques.considerations: Chemistry (pH, alcohol, acid) must work together; not just taste

clarification.title: Fining & Filtration
clarification.description: Clarification removes particles that cause haze, instability, or off-flavors. The degree of clarification is a philosophical and practical choice with implications for texture and character.

techniques.name: Natural Settling
techniques.description: Allowing particles to fall naturally by gravity over time.
techniques.method: Wine sits undisturbed in tank; particles settle to bottom; wine racked off

techniques.name: Fining
techniques.description: Adding substances that bind to particles and precipitate them out.

egg_white.character: Traditional for red Bordeaux; softens wine

techniques.vegan_concerns: Many traditional fining agents are animal-derived. Vegan wines use bentonite, PVPP, or remain unfined.

techniques.name: Filtration
techniques.description: Passing wine through a filter medium to remove particles.

crossflow.name: Crossflow Filtration
crossflow.description: Wine flows parallel to filter; gentler than perpendicular

techniques.name: Unfined & Unfiltered
techniques.description: Philosophical choice to bottle wine with minimal intervention.
techniques.trend: Increasingly common among natural wine producers and quality estates

bottling.title: Bottling
bottling.description: Bottling is the final step before wine reaches consumers. Decisions about timing, closures, and technique affect aging potential and quality.

techniques.name: Bottling Timing
techniques.description: When to bottle depends on wine style and winemaker philosophy.
considerations: Aromatic wines: Early bottling preserves freshness
Age-worthy wines: Extended aging before bottling
Market timing: Release dates may drive schedule
Vintage conditions: Some years need longer aging

techniques.name: Closures
techniques.description: The choice of closure affects aging, convenience, and perception.

natural_cork.description: Traditional bark cork from cork oak (Quercus suber)

screwcap.aging: Excellent for whites; debated for reds

crown_cap.use: Champagne during aging; natural wine for freshness

techniques.name: Bottling Line Operations
techniques.description: Modern bottling involves multiple synchronized steps.

critical_factors.oxygen: Minimize pickup through inert gas, low headspace
critical_factors.temperature: Cool wine reduces gas solubility issues

techniques.name: Bottle Shock
techniques.description: Wines often taste muted or disjointed immediately after bottling.
techniques.cause: Dissolved gases, physical disruption, temporary reduction
techniques.recommendation: Rest bottles before consumption; best estates hold wine before release

sparkling.title: Sparkling Wine Production
sparkling.description: Sparkling wine requires a second fermentation to create bubbles. The method used dramatically affects quality, texture, and style.

methods.name: Traditional Method (Methode Traditionnelle)
methods.description: Second fermentation occurs in the final bottle. The most prestigious and labor-intensive method.
process: Base wine produced (typically dry, high acid)
Aging on lees in bottle (minimum 15 months Champagne, often years)
Riddling (remuage): bottles gradually tilted to collect lees in neck
Disgorgement (degorgement): frozen plug of lees expelled
Dosage added: liqueur d'expedition sets final sweetness
methods.regions: Champagne, Cremant, Cava, Franciacorta, English sparkling, quality producers worldwide

methods.name: Tank Method (Charmat/Martinotti)
methods.description: Second fermentation occurs in pressurized tanks. Faster and more economical than traditional method.
process: Wine filtered and bottled under pressure

methods.name: Ancestral Method (Methode Ancestrale)
methods.description: Original sparkling method: wine bottled before primary fermentation completes. No second fermentation or dosage.
process: Wine may or may not be disgorged
methods.trend: Hugely popular with natural wine movement; Pet-Nat explosion since 2010s

methods.name: Transfer Method
methods.description: Hybrid: second fermentation in bottle, then transferred to tank for filtration before re-bottling.
methods.use: Half bottles, large formats where riddling is impractical

methods.name: Carbonation (Injection)
methods.description: CO2 injected into still wine under pressure.
methods.characteristics: Large, coarse bubbles; no complexity; budget sparkling

sweet.title: Sweet Wine Production
sweet.description: Great sweet wines balance concentrated sugar with acidity. Various methods concentrate sugars or arrest fermentation to achieve sweetness.

methods.name: Late Harvest
methods.description: Grapes left on vine past normal harvest to accumulate sugar through dehydration and continued ripening.
methods.character: Rich but can retain freshness; intensity depends on drying degree

methods.name: Noble Rot (Botrytis)
methods.description: Botrytis cinerea fungus in beneficial form concentrates sugars and adds honeyed complexity.
process: Specific weather conditions required (morning moisture, afternoon drying)
Botrytis perforates skins, allowing water evaporation
Sugar concentrates; botrytis adds glycerol and unique flavors
Fermentation slow; often arrested with residual sugar
methods.character: Honeyed, apricot, marmalade; waxy texture; extreme complexity

methods.name: Ice Wine (Eiswein)
methods.description: Grapes frozen on vine, pressed while frozen to concentrate sugars.
process: Grapes left on vine into winter
Water remains as ice; concentrated juice extracted
methods.character: Pure, intense fruit; high acidity balances sweetness; crystalline clarity
methods.regions: Germany, Austria, Canada (world's largest producer), Northern US
methods.challenges: Weather dependent; birds, rot risk; low yields

methods.name: Dried Grape Wines (Passito/Straw Wine)
methods.description: Grapes dried after harvest to concentrate sugars before pressing.

methods.appassimento: Grapes dried on racks or mats for weeks/months
methods.straw_wine: Dried on straw mats (Vin de Paille)

methods.name: Fortification
methods.description: Adding grape spirit to arrest fermentation, leaving residual sugar.
process: Yeast killed by alcohol; sugar remains

methods.name: Arrested Fermentation
methods.description: Stopping fermentation before all sugar is consumed, without fortification.
methods: Chilling wine to halt yeast activity
methods.examples: Many German Rieslings (Kabinett through Auslese levels)
methods.risk: Remaining yeast can restart fermentation; requires careful stabilization

fortified.title: Fortified Wines
fortified.description: Fortified wines have grape spirit added during or after fermentation. This tradition began as a preservation method for shipping but evolved into distinctive wine styles.

styles.name: Port
styles.description: Sweet fortified wine; spirit added mid-fermentation to preserve sugar.

tawny.name: Tawny Port
tawny.aging: Extended aging in small barrels; oxygen exposure

vintage.name: Vintage Port (Vintage, Single Quinta)
vintage.aging: 2-3 years in barrel, decades in bottle

white.name: White Port
white.character: From white grapes; dry to sweet

styles.production: Fermentation stopped by fortification at ~6-9% alcohol; final wine 19-22%

styles.name: Sherry
styles.description: Fortified wine aged oxidatively or under flor yeast. Immense style diversity from bone-dry to intensely sweet.

manzanilla.name: Manzanilla
manzanilla.aging: Fino aged in Sanlucar de Barrameda

palo_cortado.name: Palo Cortado
palo_cortado.aging: Mysterious: begins as Fino, evolves to Oloroso character
palo_cortado.character: Combines Amontillado aroma with Oloroso body

styles.solera: Fractional blending system: wine drawn from oldest barrels, refreshed from younger. Average age, not single vintage.

styles.name: Madeira
styles.description: Fortified wine deliberately heated during aging. Virtually indestructible; can age for centuries.

methods.estufas: Large tanks heated to 45-50°C for 3 months (basic wines)
methods.canteiro: Natural attic heat over years (premium wines)

styles.aging: 3 year (bulk), 5 year, 10 year, 15 year, 20 year, vintage (20+ years)

styles.name: Vin Doux Naturel (VDN)
styles.description: French fortified wines, spirit added mid-fermentation.

styles.muscat: Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise, Muscat de Rivesaltes - sweet, grapey

orange.title: Orange & Skin-Contact Wines
orange.description: Orange wine is white wine made like red wine: with extended skin contact. This ancient method, revived in recent decades, creates wines with amber color, tannic texture, and unique character.

techniques.name: Extended Maceration
techniques.description: White grapes fermented on their skins for days to months, extracting color, tannin, and phenolics.
techniques.fermentation: Usually spontaneous (indigenous yeast); often in amphora or neutral vessels

techniques.name: Vessel Choice
techniques.description: Container affects character significantly.

qvevri.effect: Traditional, stable temperature, specific terroir connection

techniques.name: Georgian Qvevri Wines
techniques.description: 8,000-year tradition of fermenting in buried clay vessels (qvevri).
process: Grapes (often Rkatsiteli, Mtsvane) crushed and placed in qvevri
Qvevri buried to neck; sealed with clay
Wine removed, qvevri cleaned for next vintage
techniques.unesco: Georgian qvevri winemaking inscribed on UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage list (2013)
techniques.character: Deep amber, tannic, oxidative notes, dried fruit, tea

techniques.name: Modern Orange Wine Movement
techniques.description: Revival of skin-contact whites beginning in 1990s, particularly in Slovenia and Friuli.
techniques.spread: Now produced globally from Australia to Oregon; standard category in natural wine

techniques.name: Food Pairing
techniques.description: Orange wines' structure makes them versatile with food.
techniques.serving: Slightly cool (12-16°C), not cold like typical whites

natural.title: Natural Wine
natural.description: Natural wine is a philosophy as much as a category: minimal intervention in vineyard and cellar, letting grapes express themselves without technological manipulation. Definitions vary, but the spirit emphasizes transparency and authenticity.

principles.name: The Sulfur Question
principles.description: Sulfur dioxide (SO2) use is the most contentious natural wine debate.

positions.minimal_sulfur: Small amount at bottling only (<30mg/L total)
positions.pragmatic: What's needed for stability, but minimal

principles.effects: Without sulfur, wines are more fragile, variable, and prone to faults (or funky character, depending on perspective)

principles.name: Faults vs Features
principles.description: Natural wines often show characteristics considered faults in conventional wine.
principles.perspective: Natural wine proponents argue these add character; critics call them spoilage

principles.name: Certifications & Labels
principles.description: Various attempts to define 'natural wine' officially.

examples.vin_methode_nature: French label; organic grapes, indigenous yeast, no sulfur or <30mg/L

principles.challenge: No universal definition; some prefer it that way
